chapter_summary: Dundubha, a non-venomous serpent who was formerly the ascetic Sahasrapat, narrates to Ruru why he holds his current form. In boyhood, while playing, Dundubha fashioned a fake snake out of grass and used it to frighten his brahmin friend Khagama, a fierce ascetic of great penance who was absorbed in the agnihotra fire-rite; Khagama fainted in terror. On recovering, Khagama, truthful and of fierce vows, cursed him: as he had made a powerless snake to frighten him, so by his wrath he would himself become a venomous serpent. Knowing the power of Khagama's penance, Dundubha was stricken with dread and, standing with folded hands, pleaded that it had been done only in jest and begged the curse be revoked. Moved, Khagama sighed and said his word could never prove untrue, yet gave relief: a pure son named Ruru, son of Pramati, would arise, and on seeing him the curse would end before long. Khagama then declares that Ruru is that very son and, having regained his own form, instructs him for his good. The teaching follows: non-violence (ahimsa) is the highest dharma of all living beings, and a brahmin must never harm any creature; a brahmin is gentle by birth, a knower of the Vedas and Vedangas, and a giver of fearlessness to all beings. Non-violence, truthful speech, and forgiveness are the brahmin's supreme dharma, even above the holding of the Vedas. The kshatriya's dharma of wielding the rod and protecting subjects is not for Ruru; the slaying of snakes was formerly the deed of the kshatriya Janamejaya, while the rescue of the frightened snakes came from the brahmin Astika at the snake-sacrifice.

story.title: The Curse of Khagama and the Counsel to Ruru
story.arc: A serpent reveals he was once an ascetic who, as a boy, terrified his brahmin friend with a toy snake of grass and was cursed to become a real venomous serpent. His desperate plea wins a partial reprieve: the curse will lift when he beholds the pure-born Ruru, son of Pramati. Restored to his form upon meeting Ruru, he repays the encounter by teaching Ruru that non-violence is the supreme dharma and that the brahmin's path of gentleness, truth, and forgiveness is not the kshatriya's path of the rod, illustrated by Janamejaya's snake-slaughter and Astika's rescue of the snakes.
story.timeline_note: The serpent's curse is a flashback from his boyhood as the ascetic Sahasrapat; the prophesied meeting with Ruru is the present frame. Janamejaya's sarpasatra and Astika's intervention are referenced as past events known to the listener.
story.comic_potential: A boy scaring his meditating friend with a grass snake, the friend fainting mid-ritual, then the prank backfiring into a lifelong serpent curse, has a sharp dramatic-irony beat ideal for a cautionary comic vignette.

teachings.teaching: Non-violence (ahimsa) is the highest dharma for all living beings; therefore a brahmin must never harm any creature.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework, ahimsa is not merely outward restraint but the conduct natural to a consciousness no longer driven by reactive fear and aggression; the serpent's curse springs from an act of harm born of play, showing that even careless cruelty binds, while the realized brahmin radiates fearlessness to all.

teachings.teaching: A brahmin is gentle by his very birth, a knower of the Vedas and Vedangas, and a giver of fearlessness to all beings.
teachings.lens_note: The brahmin here marks an inner stage rather than caste alone in the Kriya lens: gentleness and the power to grant fearlessness are the signs of one established in higher awareness, where knowledge of the Vedas is lived as harmlessness toward every creature.

teachings.teaching: Non-violence, truthful speech, and forgiveness together form the brahmin's supreme dharma, ranking even above the retention of the Vedas.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary prizes inner realization over mere scriptural memorization; that ahimsa, truth, and forgiveness outrank even holding the Vedas affirms that conduct flowing from a purified consciousness is higher than learning held only in memory.

teachings.teaching: The kshatriya's dharma of wielding the rod, fierceness, and protecting subjects is distinct from and not fitting for the brahmin Ruru.
teachings.lens_note: The separation of the two paths reflects, in the Kriya lens, the matching of practice to one's inner stage: the path of force belongs to a different mode of being than the brahmin's path of harmlessness, and Ruru is counselled to follow the dharma of his own nature.
